Feature Carveyance

Simplify,
Then Add Soul

The full story of the Lotus Evora — the underdog’s underrated masterpiece, the car that survived its maker’s near-death, and quite possibly the last truly analog Lotus GT.

An Appreciation  ·  2009–2021  ·  Hethel, England

I cannot quite put my finger on it, but I have always loved and rooted for Lotus — and I suspect most car enthusiasts quietly do the same. It is something about the small underdog squaring up to the giants and always, somehow, having an ace up its sleeve: a clever design trick, a chassis that shouldn’t work but does, steering the billion-dollar companies can’t seem to buy at any price. Where the rest of the industry answered every question with more power, Lotus stayed faithful to Colin Chapman’s one-line religion — simplify, then add lightness — and kept winning arguments it had no business winning.

Maybe it started for me with the Esprit in the 1980s: those folded-origami lines, the pop-up headlights, the sheer rarity of the thing. Spotting one in the wild felt like sighting Moby Dick. And that, really, is the whole Lotus paradox in a sentence. They always showed up. They were always adored by the drivers and the editors. And they never quite captured the buyers — which is perhaps exactly why they are so coveted. They were never on every corner like a Porsche. If you saw one, the person behind the wheel was different, and you found yourself wanting to walk over and ask the only question that mattered: why this, over a Corvette, a Porsche, an Audi, anything? What did they know that the rest of us didn’t? Or — better still — they knew everything the rest of us knew, and simply decided they’d rather be smiling while they drove than merely driving. That difference, the smile, is the entire point of this story.

The Unseen Hand

You’ve Been Driving Lotus All Along

Here is the open secret that makes the rooting feel justified: even when you weren’t buying a Lotus, you were very often driving one. For decades Lotus has run a quiet consultancy out of its Hethel home — Lotus Engineering — and its fingerprints are pressed into an astonishing number of cars you already love, usually doing the one thing the client couldn’t do for itself. A short list, and it could be far longer:

  1. Chevrolet Corvette ZR-1 (C4). When GM owned Lotus, it handed Hethel a clean sheet and asked for an engine worthy of a world-beating Corvette. Lotus answered with the LT5 — a 5.7-liter, four-cam, 32-valve V8 built by Mercury Marine that turned the early-’90s ZR-1 into the “King of the Hill” and embarrassed cars at twice the price.
  2. Dodge Viper. Under Chrysler’s ownership, Lotus helped turn a cast-iron truck V10 into the aluminum 8.0-liter monster at the heart of the original Viper — refining and lightening an engine that had no business feeling as alive as it did. (If you read our Three Musketeers comparison, that’s the very V10 playing Athos.)
  3. Tesla Roadster. Tesla’s entire existence rests on a Lotus. The first Roadster was built on a modified Elise chassis, assembled at Hethel — the lightweight bonded-aluminum tub that let a Silicon Valley startup get its batteries onto the road and change the industry. No Lotus, no Roadster; arguably, a very different Tesla.
  4. Isuzu. Through the 1980s and ’90s, ordinary Isuzus — the Piazza, the Impulse — wore a small badge that meant the world to enthusiasts: “Handling by Lotus.” And it wasn’t only Isuzu; Lotus’s own development legend, Roger Becker, spent time dialing in the dynamics of Toyota’s boxy 1980s Supra. The magic, it turned out, could be sprinkled onto almost anything.
  5. DeLorean DMC-12. The most famous stainless-steel car on earth was made driveable by Lotus. Chapman’s team re-engineered the DMC-12 around a backbone chassis derived from the Esprit, transforming a troubled prototype into something that could actually go down a road — and, eventually, through time.

Honorable mentions could fill a second article entirely: the Lotus Cortina and the Lotus twin-cam that powered a generation of fast Fords, the Vauxhall VX220 built on Elise bones, and on and on. The point is simple. Few companies have done more to make other companies’ cars great. Which is the first reason the Evora matters: it is one of the rare moments Lotus pointed all that accumulated genius squarely at itself.

Companion Piece · Fastest Argument in the Room

And then there is the Lotus Carlton, which deserves its own telling — and gets it in Fastest Argument in the Room. The short version: in 1990, Lotus took a humble Vauxhall Carlton family sedan, bored its straight-six out to 3.6 liters, bolted on twin turbochargers, and built a four-door that ran to 176 mph — briefly the fastest production saloon on earth, and quick enough to genuinely alarm Parliament, which debated whether a car that fast should even be legal. A grandfather’s bodyshell, a supercar’s top speed, and a Corvette ZR-1’s gearbox bolted underneath. It is the single most Lotus thing imaginable: the quiet car in the corner that turns out to be the most dangerous in the room. Read the full story →

• • •

The Gamble

Project Eagle

By the mid-2000s, Lotus was alive but not well. The Elise had saved the company in 1996 — a tiny, brilliant, bonded-aluminum featherweight that reminded the world what Chapman’s philosophy actually felt like — but the Elise’s gifts were also its ceiling. It was cramped, loud, and almost comically difficult to climb into and out of; a glorious second car for an enthusiast, and a hard sell to anyone else. Lotus didn’t just want more sales; it needed them. It needed higher margins, broader appeal, a real foothold in America, and above all a car that could be cross-shopped against a Porsche without flinching.

The trouble was philosophical. Everything that made a Lotus a Lotus came from doing without — without weight, without insulation, without compromise. How do you add refinement, space, comfort, and civility to a car whose entire soul is subtraction? Johnny Tipler’s retrospective, Lotus Evora: Speed and Style, frames the whole program around exactly this tension: a tiny, underfunded company, led back toward ambition under returning chief Mike Kimberley, gambling its future on a car that had to grow up without growing soft.

Internally it was Type 122, code-named Project Eagle, and the speed of it tells you how badly Lotus wanted in: from the first clean sheet to a finished car standing on the stand at the 2008 British International Motor Show took roughly 27 months — a blink, for an all-new car from a company this size. Its mission statement was a deliberate rewrite of Chapman’s gospel. Where the Elise said simplify, then add lightness, the Evora said something far harder: add sophistication, and somehow don’t lose the lightness. On a shoestring budget, with a fraction of Porsche’s resources, that was either madness or the most Lotus undertaking in the company’s history. It was, of course, both.

The Evora · By the Numbers

The Company That Wouldn’t Die

Born Into a Storm

Here is the part most reviews skip, and the part that makes the Evora’s steady, decade-long improvement so quietly heroic: the car was born into corporate chaos and somehow kept getting better through all of it.

It arrived for 2009, and almost immediately the company around it lost its mind. In late 2009 Lotus hired Dany Bahar, a charismatic former senior executive from Ferrari and Red Bull, to drag the brand upmarket. Within a year he unveiled, at a celebrity-stuffed 2010 Paris Motor Show, one of the most audacious plans in modern car history: a wave of five all-new Lotuses — a reborn Esprit, an Elan, an Elite, an Eterne four-door, a city car — plus a loud, expensive push into Formula 1 branding. It was thrilling. It was also wildly beyond what Hethel could afford, and the spending — private jets to grands prix, an extravagantly appointed rented house — would later become infamous.

The reckoning came fast. In 2012, Malaysian conglomerate DRB-HICOM acquired Lotus’s parent, Proton, took one look at the books, and pulled the plug on the fantasy. Bahar was suspended and then sacked; the five-car dream evaporated, leaving only the existing Elise, Exige, and Evora and a balance sheet bleeding red. For a year or two, the very survival of Lotus was an open question.

Its rescuer was a quiet, ruthless cost-cutter named Jean-Marc Gales, who arrived as CEO in 2014. Gales did the unglamorous, life-saving work: he slashed costs, streamlined the factory, stemmed the losses — and, crucially, he made a virtue of necessity. With no money for new platforms, he poured what little there was into relentlessly sharpening the cars Lotus already had. That decision is the reason the Evora’s best years came after its maker nearly died: the Evora 400, the Sport 410, and the savage GT430 are all children of the Gales era, each one a deeper, more focused reading of the original. In 2017, the Chinese giant Geely — the same patient owner that had resurrected Volvo — bought a controlling stake, and for the first time in a generation Lotus had both stability and deep pockets behind it. The Evora had survived long enough to hand the company a future.

The Evora’s best years came after its maker nearly died.

The Engineering

A Camry Engine in a Tailored Suit

The foundation was the thing Lotus understood better than anyone alive: the chassis. Rather than a heavy steel monocoque, the Evora used an evolution of the Elise’s bonded-aluminum architecture — the extruded-and-glued tub whose lineage traces to Lotus engineer Richard Rackham’s work on the Elise. Aluminum sections are pressed, bonded with aerospace epoxy and riveted into a single tub, with modular crash structures bolted to the front and rear. The result is larger, far stiffer, safer, and refined enough to carry a 2+2 grand tourer, yet still astonishingly light next to its steel-bodied rivals. And the rigidity was never an end in itself. Lotus held a near-religious conviction that suspension can only do its job if the structure beneath it refuses to flex; a stiff tub is what lets the dampers and the steering speak clearly. That chassis is why journalists kept reaching for the same word: it felt alive.

Hung at each corner were proper double wishbones, Bilstein dampers and Eibach springs, and — the decision that mattered most — a hydraulic power-steering rack, kept on faith long after most of the industry had defected to lighter, cheaper, number-dead electric systems. Lotus would not give up the one thing it did better than anyone, no matter what the efficiency spreadsheets said.

Then came the engine, and the great Lotus irony. For reliability, emissions, durability, and global parts support — all the unglamorous things a small company cannot afford to get wrong — Lotus reached for Toyota’s 3.5-liter 2GR-FE V6, an engine otherwise found humming politely beneath the hoods of Camrys, Avalons, and RAV4s, paired with a Toyota-sourced six-speed manual transaxle (an automatic, badged IPS, came later). On paper it was the least romantic choice imaginable. In practice it was genius. The naturally aspirated car made a modest 276 bhp; soon Lotus bolted on an Eaton-type supercharger for the Evora S (345 bhp) and, for the 400, added charge-cooling to take it to a full 400. Lotus rebreathed and recalibrated all of it — intake, mapping, exhaust, throttle, cooling — and gave a sensible appliance motor a voice and an urgency it never knew at the dealership. A Camry engine, in the right hands, became the beating heart of one of the finest driver’s cars of its generation: bulletproof dependability wearing a tailored Lotus suit. You could thrash it all weekend and trust it to start on Monday morning.

The Drive

Why It Exists

And now the part the whole car exists for. Drive an Evora and the conversation stops being about specifications, because the Evora’s genius isn’t on the spec sheet at all. It is in your hands and the seat of your trousers.

Start with the steering, which is the stuff of legend — and I don’t use the word loosely. Because Lotus kept faith with proper hydraulic assistance, the difference against a modern electric rack is night and day. Through the rim you feel the camber of the road, the load building and bleeding off the front tires, the exact grain of the surface beneath you, the precise instant grip begins to go. It reports all of it, constantly, like a fingertip resting on the pulse of the road. More than one seasoned reviewer reached for the highest compliment in the canon — that this was the finest steering since the Honda NSX, or a Ferrari 458 — and meant every word.

Then the ride, which may be the Evora’s quiet masterpiece. Not “good for a sports car.” Genuinely, properly beautiful. Lotus understood damping the way a great musician understands silence: the suspension breathes with the road, soaking up broken pavement progressively, keeping the tires planted, refusing to crash or jar. The result is the rarest trick in performance cars — sports-car body control wedded to luxury-car composure — and almost nobody has ever pulled it off this completely. You can cross a continent in an Evora and step out smiling, which was rather the point.

And the balance ties it all together. The Evora is mid-engined, but it never punishes you for it. It is progressive at the limit, stable under braking, and endlessly communicative — it tells you what it’s about to do long before it does it. That is the deepest expression of Chapman’s philosophy: transparency over intimidation. A lot of fast cars dare you to find their limit. The Evora takes you by the hand and walks you up to it, narrating the whole way, so that an ordinary driver feels like a hero and a great one feels like a god.

It tells you what it’s about to do long before it does it.

None of this was an accident, and to understand where it came from you have to meet the family that built the feel. For 43 years, the way a Lotus drove was largely the work of one man: Roger Becker, who joined the company in 1966 as a trimmer on the Europa line and was plucked onto Chapman’s fledgling dynamics team the moment his talent at the wheel became obvious. Becker is the reason Lotus has a reputation for ride and handling at all — and, gloriously, he is also the man behind one of the great car-chase legends in cinema. When the official stunt driver couldn’t wring enough drama out of the white Esprit for The Spy Who Loved Me — the car simply had too much grip — Becker, who’d only come along to deliver the cars, climbed in and slid it up a Sardinian hillside on opposite lock. The producers tore up the plan and put him in the film, doubling for Roger Moore for seven weeks (some scenes shot at speed in reverse, looking forward through the mirror). Chapman’s affectionate nickname for him, after a prototype ended up in a ditch, was “Bodger Wrecker.” Roger Becker retired in 2009 — the very year the Evora launched, the last car he ever signed off.

The dynamics baton passed, with a storybook neatness, to his son. Matt Becker grew up in the passenger seats of development Lotuses, watching his father wiggle cars down wet roads to read the steering, and began his own Lotus apprenticeship at 16 (on the same day, as it happens, as future Lotus and McLaren engineering bosses). The Elise had been a development of something that already existed; the Evora was Matt’s first true clean sheet — the car, by his own account, that defined his career — and he set out to build something as agile as an Elise but civilised enough to live with over long distances, a Lotus you wouldn’t dread on a five-hour drive. He tuned its steering and its ride personally. (He has been candid, too, that the Evora was never meant to be a 911 — and that Porsche’s ergonomics taught him plenty.) In 2014 he left to become chief vehicle-attribute engineer at Aston Martin, carrying that Lotus sense of feel with him. Father and son, the Esprit and the Evora: the whole arc of how a Lotus learned to talk to its driver, bookended by one family.

And it all happened at Hethel, where Lotus has always behaved less like an automaker than a racing team — tiny crews, thin budgets, constant improvisation, engineers wearing six hats at once and prototyping on instinct. Russell Carr’s design aged with uncommon grace precisely because it chased proportion instead of gimmicks. Passion-built, by a small group of people who cared rather more than the budget strictly allowed. You can feel that, too.

Growing Up

The Suitcase and the Soul

For all its soul, the Evora had a commercial job to do, and that meant living in the real world. To pull a buyer away from a Cayman or a 911, Lotus had to offer things it had historically treated as beneath it: seats you could actually sit in, doors you could get through with dignity, climate control, navigation, somewhere to put a bag, a cabin that didn’t roar. The Evora delivered a more premium dashboard, better ergonomics, real materials — and, symbolically, those famous tiny rear seats.

Let’s be honest about the 2+2: nobody is touring Europe with two adults in the back. But the seats mattered all the same, because of what they declared. They turned the Evora from a weekend toy into a genuine grand tourer — a car you could load with luggage, take the kids in, drive across a country and daily through a city. For Lotus, that was nothing short of revolutionary. Tipler’s book doesn’t pretend the transformation was flawless; Lotus still wrestled with fit-and-finish consistency, the sort of detail polish that comes naturally to a company building a million cars a year and painfully to one building a few thousand by hand, often with switchgear borrowed from the corporate parts bin. But the ambition was real, and mostly, remarkably, it landed.

The Bloodline

Sharpening One Blade

Then Lotus did the most Lotus thing of all: rather than reinvent the Evora every few years, it simply kept sharpening the same blade. The story of the variants is the story of a single idea, honed obsessively over twelve years.

The original naturally aspirated Evora (2009) is, to many, the sweetest of them all — 276 bhp, around 1,350 kg, softer-edged, the most delicate and beautifully balanced reading of the idea, and the only one that asks you to actually work the gorgeous chassis to get the best from it. The Evora S (2010) added the supercharger and the 345-bhp midrange urgency the NA car politely lacked, turning a graceful GT into a properly quick one; this era also brought the IPS automatic, a 414E hybrid technology demonstrator, and a competition career — the Evora GTE race cars made up to 470 bhp and took a class podium at Le Mans in 2011. (Italy’s Carabinieri even ran a pair as patrol cars, which is the most cinematic traffic stop imaginable.)

The great reboot was the Evora 400 (2015) — the first fruit of the Gales turnaround and the car that brought Lotus back to America. A new charge-cooled supercharger lifted it to 400 bhp at 7,000 rpm and 302 lb-ft; it shed 42 kg, gained a far better interior and easier ingress, and Lotus claimed the majority of the car was new. It hit 60 mph in 4.1 seconds and ran to 186 mph. The Sport 410 (2016) went lighter and harder: 410 bhp, a full 70 kg trimmed from the 400 (carbon roof, splitter and rear deck; even the badges swapped for stickers), 0–60 in 4.0 seconds and 190 mph. For Lotus’s 70th anniversary it was reworked into the more touring-minded GT410.

And then the summit: the Evora GT430 (2017). At 430 bhp and 325 lb-ft, on a dry weight of just 1,258 kg, it was the most powerful road car Lotus had ever built — carbon aero generating up to 250 kg of downforce, two-way adjustable Öhlins dampers, AP Racing brakes, Michelin Cup 2 tires, 0–60 in around 3.7 seconds. Lotus built just 60. Its stablemate, the wingless GT430 Sport — another 60 cars — traded downforce for slipperiness and became, at 196 mph, the fastest production Lotus of its era. Many enthusiasts consider the GT430 the greatest road-going Lotus ever made. The North American farewell, the 2020 Evora GT (around 416 bhp, 0–60 in 3.8 seconds), sent the car off in the only market that had given Porsche its hardest fight. Across all of it — every variant, every year — just 6,117 Evoras left Hethel. Evolution, never revolution; the patient, obsessive refinement of a company that knew it had gotten the bones right the first time.

Against the Establishment

Engineered vs. Handcrafted

It was, of course, forever measured against the inevitable benchmark, and the comparison is illuminating precisely because the Evora kept losing on paper and winning in the heart. Against the Porsche Cayman and 911, the Lotus conceded the interior, the dealer network, the polished electronics, the unimpeachable build quality. Porsche, as ever, felt engineered — flawless, clinical, devastatingly competent. The Evora felt handcrafted — warmer, more tactile, more alive, more willing to talk to you at sane speeds rather than only at ten-tenths. Its own dynamics engineer freely admitted the Evora was never trying to be a 911; it was trying to be something Porsche had largely engineered out of its cars — an unfiltered conversation between road and hands. Against the Audi R8 it gave away drama and luxury but felt smaller, lighter, more intimate. And against its own siblings, the Elise and Exige, it was simply more grown-up — more usable, more complete — without, the enthusiasts will insist, being any less of a Lotus. It wasn’t a diluted Lotus. It was a broader one.

The Tragedy

Beloved, and Overlooked

Which makes what happened next quietly heartbreaking. The Evora never troubled the sales charts — 6,117 cars in twelve years, a figure Porsche can clear in a couple of months of Caymans. The reasons were many and mostly unfair: a brand the wider public wasn’t sure about, made shakier still by the Bahar circus; a thin and inconsistent dealer network; almost no marketing budget; a price that bumped against Porsche’s; and a lingering perception — fair or not — that a Lotus might rattle. Worse, regulatory headaches around airbag rules forced Lotus out of the United States entirely in the mid-2010s, surrendering its single biggest opportunity until the Evora 400 finally brought it back in 2016. Editors adored it; buyers walked past it to the showroom they trusted. And so the Evora became the thing the best Lotuses always seem to become: beloved by the few, overlooked by the many, and therefore coveted by anyone who knew. Exactly the Moby Dick paradox from the start of this piece, lived out one more time.

What It Meant

The Last Analog Lotus

The Evora’s reward came in its inheritance. Its grand-touring mission, its everyday usability, its broader ambitions — all of it flowed directly into the Emira, the car that succeeded it under the new, deep-pocketed Geely ownership and became the final pure-combustion Lotus the company will ever build (the future, in the shape of the electric Evija hypercar and the Eletre SUV, is already here). The Emira is, in many ways, the more polished machine. But ask the enthusiasts and they’ll tell you the Evora still feels lighter, more delicate, more mechanically pure — which is why it is increasingly spoken of as the last truly analog Lotus GT, the end of a line that runs straight back to Chapman and to Roger Becker sliding an Esprit up a Sardinian hill.

That is the deeper meaning of the Evora, and the reason it deserves your attention now even more than when it was new. It proved something most companies never manage: that you can chase refinement without surrendering your soul. Nearly everyone loses their character on the road to becoming respectable. The Evora grew up, learned manners, packed a suitcase, took on the giants, survived its own maker’s near-death — and stayed, unmistakably, a Lotus. Not the fastest. Not the most luxurious. Not the most advanced. But perhaps the best-balanced, best-riding, best-steering, and most emotionally honest driver’s car of its era.

Which brings us back to the smile. That was always the difference, wasn’t it — the thing the Evora’s owner understood that the rest of us were still working out. The Porsche driver gets where they’re going, flawlessly. The Lotus driver gets where they’re going grinning like an idiot, having felt every inch of the road through their fingertips, in a car the whole industry quietly helped build but only Lotus could have given a soul. They didn’t know less than we did. They knew the one thing that matters most: that the point was never simply to drive. The point was to be glad you did.
